Patch Set 7:

(1 comment)

Was any performance testing done with this patch for HDD-based systems under load?  I was just thinking about why the DMU code was originally written to do prefetching, and clearly the benefit of that depends on the latency of the storage and how many threads are accessing it concurrently.

On a highly-concurrent system (e.g. OSS with hundreds of threads doing bulk writes) on HDDs there would very likely be a benefit to prefetching metadata asynchronously to allow the IO scheduler to aggregate multiple requests at once, while with SSD/NVRAM there is very low latency for accessing cold data blocks so prefetching would have minimal value.

That makes me wonder if this patch will hurt HDD OSS performance, or do we hide the access latency in Lustre by already having many OSS threads active at once?

That also makes me wonder if it makes sense to check (in a separate patch) the /sys/block/sdX/queue/rotational value (HDD=1, SSD=0) and internally disable prefetch in the DMU/SPA as wasted effort and potentially get an overall efficiency gain?  There is already a vdev_nonrot flag internally, but it is only used for block ordering in the IO scheduler, not to avoid submitting prefetch requests entirely.

Patch Set 7:

Andreas, what kind of metadata are you concern of? OI? indirect blocks? this patch doesn't touch osd_declare_write_commit() which is used to declare writes for OST_WRITE.

Patch Set 7:

Alex, I was thinking about the use of declare statements that check specific arguments (e.g. presence of some entry in a ZAP, or some indirect block) to be used to prefetch such ZFS internal metadata into memory, and if we remove such checks it may impact performance on HDDs more than in your current RAM-based testing.

(2 comments)

Patch Set 7:

(2 comments)

sorry, still thinking how to address your concern on declarations w/o prefetching.
i'm pretty sure about few cases (like last_rcvd and llogs) where we've been overwriting rougly same blocks all time time. less sure about OI (still thinking).
_probably_ it makes sense to benchmark again with the patches Matt developed and see how much we save with the patch.

it took too long.. but I just realized that actually declarations in the question don't do any sort of prefetching. e.g. dmu_tx_hold_zap() does full lookup, dmu_tx_hold_write() reads with zio_wait(), etc.

commit.subject: LU-7898 osd: remove unnecessary declarations
commit.message:
LU-7898 osd: remove unnecessary declarations

Refactor the code a bit to remove unnecessary declarations
(which are very expensive in ZFS). The patch also introduces
initial preparations to support large dnodes - it tracks
all declared EAs at object creation and tracked number can
be used to request dnode of appropriate size.

With this patch + LU-7918 disk/memory space reserved for a
single-stripe creation goes down from ~33MB to 4.6MB.

Performance improvements from this patch are also significant.
Running mdtest create performance on a test node (ramdisk):

    Threads    0.6.5   0.6.5+patch
        1       9933       14279
        2      12870       20469
        4      16405       26407
        8      19320       28254
